questions.stem: A 65-year-old male smoker presents with a 2-week history of progressive facial swelling, dyspnoea on exertion, and distended neck veins. He has no chest pain or cough. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of facial swelling, dyspnoea, and distended neck veins (from elevated venous pressure) is classic for SVC obstruction, often due to lung cancer. Tamponade gives muffled heart sounds and hypotension; pulmonary embolism presents with acute pleuritic chest pain and hypoxia; aortic dissection causes severe tearing pain; anaphylaxis has urticaria and wheeze.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with Hodgkin lymphoma on ABVD chemotherapy reports mild facial puffiness and visible veins on her chest over the past week. There is no dysphagia, stridor, or fever. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Facial puffiness with dilated chest veins points to SVC obstruction, commonly from mediastinal lymphoma. Hypersensitivity typically includes rash and itch; lymphangitic carcinomatosis causes dyspnoea and cough; radiation fibrosis takes months to develop; hypothyroidism gives generalized oedema and fatigue.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with known non-small cell lung cancer presents with acute dyspnoea, inspiratory stridor, facial swelling, and distended neck veins. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Superior vena cava obstruction with tracheal compression
questions.explanation: The combination of SVC obstruction signs (facial swelling, distended veins) with stridor indicates a mediastinal mass also compressing the trachea. Asthma lacks venous distension; anaphylaxis has urticaria and hypotension; tamponade has pulsus paradoxus; pulmonary embolism has no stridor.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer and a tunnelled central line for chemotherapy presents with acute onset of right arm and facial swelling over 2 hours. There is no stridor or chest pain. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Superior vena cava thrombosis from indwelling catheter
questions.explanation: Acute onset in the presence of a central line suggests catheter-related thrombosis of the SVC. Tumour progression is more gradual; infection would have fever and erythema; lymphoedema is not acute; pulmonary embolism would cause dyspnoea and hypoxia.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with no significant medical history presents to the emergency department with acute facial and periorbital swelling after eating lunch. She reports mild dyspnoea but no urticaria, wheeze, or pruritus. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Superior vena cava obstruction from undiagnosed mediastinal mass
questions.explanation: Absence of allergic symptoms (urticaria, wheeze) makes angioedema or anaphylaxis less likely. Acute facial swelling with dyspnoea can be the first presentation of SVC obstruction due to a mediastinal lymphoma or other mass. Cushing's and nephrotic syndrome are chronic.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with a 2-week history of facial puffiness and distended neck veins. She has no respiratory symptoms. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis of superior vena cava (SVC) obstruction?
questions.explanation: CT chest with contrast is the gold standard for confirming SVC obstruction, as it directly visualizes the obstructed SVC and collateral circulation, and can identify the underlying cause. Chest X-ray may show mediastinal widening but is not diagnostic.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with known non-small cell lung cancer develops mild facial swelling and exertional dyspnoea. A CT chest with contrast shows a mediastinal mass but only subtle narrowing of the SVC. What is the most appropriate next investigation to definitively confirm SVC obstruction?
options: Doppler ultrasound of the internal jugular veins
Repeat CT chest with contrast in 3 months
questions.explanation: MR venography is a non-invasive, accurate method to confirm SVC obstruction when CT is equivocal, by directly visualizing the SVC and collaterals. Conventional venography is invasive and rarely needed. Doppler ultrasound is less sensitive for the entire SVC.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with metastatic lung cancer presents with acute-onset facial swelling, stridor, and cyanosis. A chest X-ray is performed. Which chest X-ray finding is most specific for SVC obstruction?
questions.explanation: A prominent azygos vein shadow indicates a dilated azygos vein acting as a collateral, which is highly specific for SVC obstruction. Widened mediastinum is common but not specific. Right hilar mass may be the cause but not diagnostic of obstruction.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old patient with SVC obstruction due to small cell lung cancer develops worsening headache and blurred vision. You suspect thrombotic extension of the SVC obstruction. Which investigation is most appropriate to assess for thrombus?
options: Doppler ultrasound of the neck and upper chest
questions.explanation: Doppler ultrasound of the neck and upper chest can visualize thrombus in the SVC and its branches, is non-invasive, and readily available. D-dimer is non-specific. CT head may show cerebral edema but not thrombus directly.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old child with a central venous line in the right internal jugular vein develops facial swelling and dilated neck veins. Which is the most appropriate first-line investigation to confirm SVC obstruction in this child?
questions.explanation: Doppler ultrasound is first-line in children to avoid radiation and sedation; it can demonstrate absence of flow or thrombus in the SVC and jugular veins. CT involves radiation, and MR often requires sedation.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with a 2-week history of facial swelling, dyspnoea, and distended neck veins. CT chest reveals a mediastinal mass causing superior vena cava obstruction. Biopsy confirms small cell lung cancer (limited stage). His performance status is 1. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: Small cell lung cancer is highly chemosensitive; chemotherapy with etoposide and a platinum agent is first-line treatment for SCLC presenting with SVC obstruction, as it treats both the underlying cancer and the obstruction. Stenting is typically reserved for patients who fail to respond to chemotherapy or have severe, life-threatening symptoms.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with stage IIIB non-small cell lung cancer presents with acute onset of severe stridor, facial cyanosis, and confusion. CT venogram shows near-complete occlusion of the superior vena cava. She is not on anticoagulation. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Urgent chemotherapy with cisplatin plus gemcitabine
questions.explanation: In severe, life-threatening SVC obstruction with stridor and cerebral symptoms, immediate mechanical relief via endovascular stenting is the most appropriate first-line treatment. It provides rapid symptom resolution. Radiotherapy and chemotherapy take longer to work, and steroids are adjunctive, not definitive.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with Hodgkin lymphoma presents with swelling of the face and arms. CT demonstrates a mediastinal mass causing SVC obstruction. He has no airway compromise. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: Hodgkin lymphoma is highly chemosensitive. For patients with SVC obstruction due to lymphoma, chemotherapy (such as ABVD) is first-line treatment as it addresses the underlying disease and typically leads to rapid reduction of the mass. Stenting is reserved for those with severe, refractory symptoms or contraindications to chemotherapy.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer and SVC obstruction is undergoing chemotherapy. He develops worsening facial swelling. A CT venogram reveals a new thrombus in the SVC. He has no contraindications to anticoagulation. What is the most appropriate additional treatment?
options: Therapeutic anticoagulation with low molecular weight heparin
questions.explanation: The presence of an SVC thrombus in the setting of SVC obstruction requires anticoagulation to prevent progression and embolization. Low molecular weight heparin is first-line for cancer-associated thrombosis. Stenting may be considered if anticoagulation alone fails, but immediate management is anticoagulation.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man presents with SVC obstruction due to a large mediastinal mass. Biopsy reveals a germ cell tumour (non-seminoma). He has mild symptoms. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: Germ cell tumours are highly chemosensitive, and BEP chemotherapy is the standard first-line treatment for mediastinal germ cell tumours. This treats the underlying malignancy and will resolve the SVC obstruction. Stenting is rarely needed upfront.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with known Hodgkin lymphoma presents to the emergency department with a 2-week history of progressive facial swelling and breathlessness. On examination, she has facial plethora, distended neck veins, and a blood pressure of 100/70 mmHg with a pulsus paradoxus of 12 mmHg. Her oxygen saturations are 94% on room air. Chest X-ray shows a wide mediastinum. What is the single most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Insert a femoral central venous line
Arrange urgent CT chest with contrast
questions.explanation: The patient has signs of cardiac tamponade (pulsus paradoxus, hypotension) complicating SVC obstruction. The most immediate life-threatening issue is tamponade, so an emergency echocardiogram is needed to confirm and guide pericardiocentesis. CT chest is important but not the immediate priority. Steroids are for SVC obstruction but not if tamponade is suspected. Furosemide may worsen preload in tamponade. Central line is not urgent and could be dangerous in SVC obstruction.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with small cell lung cancer presents with a 3-day history of swelling of his face and arms, and coughing. He is hemodynamically stable with saturations 97% on air. He has mild stridor at rest. CT chest shows a mediastinal mass compressing the SVC. He has no prior treatment. What is the single most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Perform a CT-guided biopsy of the mass
questions.explanation: In stable SVC obstruction with airway compromise (stridor), corticosteroids (dexamethasone) should be given first to reduce edema and inflammation. Definitive treatment (chemotherapy, radiotherapy, stenting) can be planned once the diagnosis is confirmed. Biopsy may be needed but the immediate action is to give steroids. Stenting is reserved for severe or refractory cases. Chemotherapy/radiotherapy are not immediate steps.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer on palliative chemotherapy presents with rapid onset of facial swelling and dyspnoea. She has distended neck veins and a blood pressure of 85/50 mmHg, heart rate 120 bpm. She is awake but distressed. CT chest confirms SVC obstruction with a large mediastinal mass. What is the single most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Insert a urinary catheter to monitor output
Call the interventional radiologist for urgent stenting
questions.explanation: This patient is hypotensive and tachycardic due to SVC obstruction with reduced preload. In an unstable patient, urgent endovascular stenting is the fastest way to relieve obstruction and restore hemodynamic stability. IV fluids may worsen edema; steroids work too slowly; furosemide reduces preload and is contraindicated. Catheter is not immediate.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with a history of non-small cell lung cancer, recently started on immunotherapy, presents with mild facial puffiness and cough for 1 week. He is euvolemic, saturations 98%, and has no stridor. CT shows a small mediastinal mass with 30% SVC narrowing. He has good venous collaterals on examination. What is the single most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Request a CT venogram for further assessment
Insert a peripherally inserted central catheter (PICC)
Reassure and arrange outpatient oncology follow-up
questions.explanation: This patient has mild, well-compensated SVC obstruction with collaterals. In the absence of severe symptoms or progression, immediate intervention is not required. Reassurance and outpatient follow-up with the oncology team is appropriate. Steroids would be over-treatment. Radiotherapy and stenting are reserved for significant obstruction. CT venogram is unnecessary. PICC insertion is not indicated.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with no past medical history presents to ED with a 3-week history of progressive facial swelling and distended neck veins. She has a palpable left supraclavicular lymph node. Her oxygen saturations are 97% on air. CT chest shows a large anterior mediastinal mass with SVC compression. She is stable. What is the single most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Refer for urgent mediastinoscopy and biopsy
Biopsy the supraclavicular lymph node under local anaesthetic
Start chemotherapy after tumour board discussion
questions.explanation: In a stable patient with SVC obstruction and a palpable peripheral lymph node, the most appropriate immediate action is to biopsy that node to obtain a histological diagnosis. Starting treatment without a diagnosis (chemotherapy, steroids, stenting) is not appropriate. Mediastinoscopy is more invasive and reserved if no peripheral node is available.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with recently diagnosed small cell lung cancer presents with facial swelling and distended neck veins. Which clinical finding most strongly suggests imminent airway compromise requiring urgent intervention?
questions.explanation: Stridor indicates upper airway obstruction from laryngeal edema, an immediate life-threatening complication of SVC obstruction. Hoarseness may occur but does not signal acute airway compromise.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with known mediastinal lymphoma and mild facial puffiness is being managed conservatively. She now reports a new, persistent headache that is worse when lying flat. Which symptom should prompt urgent imaging of the head?
questions.explanation: Headache, especially worse on lying down, suggests raised intracranial pressure from impaired venous drainage, a dangerous complication of SVC obstruction requiring urgent imaging. Nausea and dizziness are non-specific.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with non-small cell lung cancer and established SVC obstruction presents with acute dyspnoea, facial swelling, and engorged neck veins. His oxygen saturations are 94% on air. Which additional finding indicates life-threatening obstruction with risk of cerebral hypoperfusion?
questions.explanation: Confusion implies reduced cerebral perfusion due to markedly elevated venous pressure, a critical red flag. Cyanosis and accessory muscle use are important but confusion is the most imminent sign of cerebral compromise.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with a history of breast cancer and known SVC obstruction develops acute severe headache, blurred vision, and focal seizures. What is the most likely life-threatening complication that must be excluded immediately?
questions.explanation: Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis is a known complication of SVC obstruction due to hypercoagulability and venous stasis, presenting with headache, visual changes, and seizures. It requires urgent imaging and anticoagulation.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old man with recently diagnosed lung cancer complains of mild facial swelling and hoarseness. On examination, you ask him to raise both arms. Which finding would confirm the diagnosis of SVC obstruction?
options: Appearance of collateral veins on the chest
Development of facial plethora and cyanosis
questions.explanation: Pemberton's sign (facial congestion and cyanosis upon arm elevation) is pathognomonic for SVC obstruction due to thoracic inlet compression. While other signs like JVP increase are common, Pemberton's is the classic provocative test.
